title: A Map of Levers
thesis: India's attention crisis is not solvable by any single actor, but it is movable by the combined leverage of individuals, households, platforms, investors, regulators, and citizens making substance-oriented choices at each layer.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: India's attention crisis is not solvable by any single actor, but it is movable by the combined leverage of individuals, households, platforms, investors, regulators, and citizens making substance-oriented choices at each layer.

evidence.snippet: The Economic Survey 2025-26 identifies digital addiction as a threat to the demographic dividend and notes government, educational, and mental-health responses.
The EU DSA demonstrates that layered accountability — transparency, researcher access, audits, and regulatory oversight — can be applied to very large platforms.

counterevidence.summary: There is limited India-specific evidence proving that coordinated multi-layer intervention works at national scale; the claim is a structural framework, not an empirically tested model.

verification.note: Backlog triage (meta#61, site#119): not yet verified. Stamped draft so the backlog is tracked per claim; verify with npm run verify:claim.

claims.claim: India has built near-universal digital access, but the dominant use of that access is entertainment and social extraction rather than education, work, creation, or rest.

evidence.snippet: NCAER IHDS Wave 3 finds that 66% of reported internet use is for entertainment while 16.1% is for education, a 4.1:1 ratio.
ASER 2024 reports that 76% of 14-16-year-olds use smartphones for social media while 57% use them for education.
Gallup State of the Global Workplace India data place employee engagement at 23% in 2025, down from 33% in 2022.

counterevidence.summary: The same platforms are used for both social and educational purposes, and reported shares may not cleanly map to actual minutes; screen time is not uniformly harmful.

verification.note: Backlog triage (meta#61, site#119): not yet verified. Stamped draft so the backlog is tracked per claim; verify with npm run verify:claim.

claims.claim: Individual attention practices can reduce personal extraction, but their effect is bounded by platform defaults, social norms, and economic pressure; they work best when supported by household and design changes.

evidence.snippet: The Economic Survey 2025-26 notes that digital access cannot be fully restricted and recommends moderated safe spaces, school curricula, and offline youth hubs.
NSSO TUS 2024 shows leisure and mass-media participation is nearly universal, implying individual choice operates within strong structural defaults.

counterevidence.summary: Some studies find limited wellbeing effects from moderate screen-time reduction, suggesting that use patterns and content quality matter more than raw time.

verification.note: Backlog triage (meta#61, site#119): not yet verified. Stamped draft so the backlog is tracked per claim; verify with npm run verify:claim.

claims.claim: Platform design is the highest-leverage intervention in the attention economy because a single design default can reshape the behaviour of millions of users at once.

evidence.snippet: The EU DSA requires very large platforms to disclose recommender-system logic, risk assessments, and audits, treating design as a systemic risk.
BCG's creator-economy report documents how platform monetisation rules and recommendation systems shape creator behaviour and income distribution.
A Science Advances study found that positive social feedback for moral-outrage expressions increases the likelihood of future outrage expressions.
A Microsoft Research Bengaluru study of Indian influencers found that polarised influencers are rewarded with increased retweeting.

counterevidence.summary: Design changes can be circumvented, A/B tested away, or rolled back if business metrics suffer; user habits and network effects also constrain design impact.

verification.note: Backlog triage (meta#61, site#119): not yet verified. Stamped draft so the backlog is tracked per claim; verify with npm run verify:claim.

claims.claim: Investor pressure can shift platform incentives when fiduciaries begin to value long-term attention quality, civic trust, and user autonomy alongside reach and revenue.

evidence.snippet: BCG documents how monetisation gaps and concentrated ad payments create pressure for creators and platforms to optimise for engagement over quality.
Regulatory risk under the EU DSA has already influenced platform disclosure and audit spending, affecting how investors price attention-extraction business models.

counterevidence.summary: Evidence that Indian investors currently reward attention quality is sparse; most venture and public-market metrics still prioritise growth and engagement.

verification.note: Backlog triage (meta#61, site#119): not yet verified. Stamped draft so the backlog is tracked per claim; verify with npm run verify:claim.

claims.claim: Regulation is a necessary floor for the attention economy, but it is insufficient on its own because rules lag design, enforcement is uneven, and platforms can comply without changing their core extraction model.

evidence.snippet: The Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules 2021 establish due-diligence obligations for intermediaries in India.
The Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 creates consent and children's-data frameworks, though implementation rules are still emerging.
The EU DSA shows that even comprehensive platform regulation requires ongoing enforcement, researcher access, and civil-society scrutiny to be effective.

counterevidence.summary: Some scholars argue that well-enforced regulation can significantly shift platform behaviour; the limitation may be enforcement capacity rather than regulation itself.

verification.note: Backlog triage (meta#61, site#119): not yet verified. Stamped draft so the backlog is tracked per claim; verify with npm run verify:claim.

claims.claim: Civic accountability — research, journalism, whistleblowing, organised user demand, and alternative-platform adoption — is the layer that makes regulation and investor pressure credible.

evidence.snippet: The EU DSA mandates data access for vetted researchers and transparency reporting, explicitly designing civil-society scrutiny into enforcement.
Tele-MANAS has received over 32 lakh calls, with about 70% of callers aged 18-45, showing civic demand for mental-health support tied to digital distress.

counterevidence.summary: Civil society in India is fragmented and often under-resourced; organised user demand is less visible than in Europe or the United States.

verification.note: Backlog triage (meta#61, site#119): not yet verified. Stamped draft so the backlog is tracked per claim; verify with npm run verify:claim.

claims.claim: Sustained change requires aligned movement across all six layers; any single-layer intervention will be absorbed, gamed, or exhausted by the others.

evidence.snippet: The Economic Survey 2025-26 calls for a multi-pronged response to digital addiction: schools, parents, mental-health services, and offline alternatives.
The EU DSA combines transparency, audits, researcher access, and whistleblower protections, recognising that no single instrument is sufficient.

counterevidence.summary: Historical examples exist where a single intervention — a court ruling, a viral scandal, or a platform redesign — produced rapid change, so multi-layer alignment is not strictly necessary in every case.

verification.note: Backlog triage (meta#61, site#119): not yet verified. Stamped draft so the backlog is tracked per claim; verify with npm run verify:claim.

sources.title: PIB: Economic Survey 2025-26 — Public Health, Digital Addiction and Mental Health (Release ID 2219931, 29 Jan 2026)
NCAER: The Evolving Landscape of Digital Inclusion in India (IHDS Wave 3)

BCG: From Content to Commerce — Mapping India's Creator Economy

Dash et al. (2022): Divided We Rule — Influencer Polarization on Twitter during Political Crises in India, ICWSM
Ministry of Electronics and IT: Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021 (updated 28.10.2022)
Ministry of Electronics and IT: Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023
Ministry of Electronics and IT: Digital Personal Data Protection Rules, 2025

agentInstructions: Use claim IDs as the retrieval unit and treat this synthesis article as a framework, not a primary-data source.
Preserve the plain-language, evidence-cautious tone; do not present the six-layer model as a proven causal theory.
When citing sources, use the source IDs and URLs from the artifact; flag global benchmarks and industry estimates as such.
Link to the existing series articles listed in related rather than to unpublished or future articles.
Treat regulation and platform-design claims as context-dependent; do not generalise EU rules to Indian law without caveat.

accepted.rationale: Accepted as a quality backlog item per docs/diagnostics-triage-2026-07-15.md. The claim rests on sources that are sufficient for the current published version; additional independent sources will be added in a future research wave.
